content.problemStatement:
A permutation is an array consisting of NNN distinct integers from 111 to NNN in arbitrary order.

Chef has two permutation arrays AAA and PPP of length NNN. Chef performs the following three types of operations on the array AAA:


111 : Permute the array AAA according to the order defined by permutation PPP.That is, replace AAA with the array BBB where B[P[i]]=A[i]B[P[i]]=A[i]B[P[i]]=A[i].
2 x y2 \ x \ y2 x y : Swap the elements at positions xxx and yyy in AAA.
3 x3 \ x3 x : Output the element at position xxx in array AAA.
For each operation of type 333, Chef wants you to output the element at position xxx in array AAA after all preceding operations have been applied.

###Input


The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN integers denoting elements of array AAA.
The third line contains NNN integers denoting elements of array PPP.
The fourth line contains a single integer QQQ, denoting the number of operations to be performed.
QQQ lines follow, each describing an operation of one of the three types above.
###Output
For each test case, for each query of type 333, output the answer in a separate line.

###Constraints


1≤T≤10001 \leq T \leq 10001≤T≤1000
1≤N,Q≤1051 \leq N, Q \leq 10^51≤N,Q≤105
1≤A[i],P[i],x,y≤N1 \leq A[i],P[i], x, y \leq N1≤A[i],P[i],x,y≤N
AAA and PPP are permutations.
x≠yx\ne yx=y in type 222 operations.
The sum of NNN and the sum of QQQ over all test cases do not exceed 10510^5105.
constraints: A and P are permutations of {1,…,N}
The sum of N over all test cases ≤ 100000
The sum of Q over all test cases ≤ 100000
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line N, the size of the permutations. Second line N integers A[1..N] – the initial permutation. Third line N integers P[1..N] – a permutation describing the re‑ordering operation. Fourth line Q, the number of operations. Then Q lines follow, each describing an operation:
1                     – apply the permutation P to the current array A (A becomes B where B[P[i]] = A[i] for all i).
2 x y                 – swap the elements at positions x and y in the current array.
3 x                   – output the element at position x in the current array.
All indices are 1‑based.
content.outputFormat: For each operation of type 3, output the requested element on its own line, in the order the queries appear.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Initially, array AAA is [1,2,3][1,2,3][1,2,3].
It changes as follows: [1,2,3][1,2,3][1,2,3] -> [3,1,2][3,1,2][3,1,2] -> [3,2,1][3,2,1][3,2,1].
So, we output element at position 111 in array AAA, which is 333.

solution.approach: Let the fixed permutation be P and its inverse be invP. After applying the operation of type 1 t times, an element originally at position i moves to position P^t[i]; consequently, the element currently at position x originated from position invP^t[x]. We keep the original array A0 unchanged and maintain a counter t (initially 0). For a query or a swap we need invP^t[x] for the involved positions. Using binary lifting we pre‑compute upInv[k][i] = position obtained by applying invP 2^k times to i (k up to 17 because t ≤ Q ≤ 1e5). Then invP^t[x] is obtained by scanning the bits of t and jumping through upInv – O(log t) time. Type 1 simply increments t, type 2 swaps A0[invP^t[x]] and A0[invP^t[y]], and type 3 outputs A0[invP^t[x]]. This yields O((N+Q)·log N) time overall and O(N·log N) memory for the lifting table.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + Q) · log N) per test case (log N ≤ 17 for the given limits).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N · log N) for the binary‑lifting table plus O(N) for the array.
